title: Variables & Data Types
subtitle: Give your values names and learn the four kinds of stuff Python can hold.
goal: Store values in variables using =, choose good names, and tell apart the four core types: int, float, str, and bool.

blocks.heading: What is a variable? 📦
blocks.html:
Imagine a box with a sticky label on the front. You put something inside the box, write a name on the label, and now you can find it again later just by saying the name.

In Python, that box is called a variable. It stores a value (a number, some text, anything) and gives it a name so you can use it over and over.

blocks.heading: Making a variable with =
blocks.html:
To make a variable, you write a name, then an equals sign =, then the value you want to store.

The = here does not mean "is equal to" like in math. It means "put this value into this box." Python reads the right side first, then drops it into the label on the left.

blocks.caption: Storing a value, then using it
blocks.code:
age = 10
print(age)
print("My age is")
print(age)
blocks.explain: We put 10 into a box labeled age. After that, writing age anywhere means "give me what's in the box" — which is 10.

blocks.html: Notice there are no quotes around age when we print it. Quotes mean "plain text." No quotes means "look inside the box with this name."

blocks.heading: Naming your boxes well 🏷️
blocks.html:
You can name a variable almost anything, but there are a few rules and one good habit:

  • Use snake_case: lowercase words joined by underscores, like favorite_color.
  • No spaces allowed — my age breaks, my_age works.
  • It can't start with a number — 2cool breaks, cool2 works.
  • Pick names that explain themselves: score is way better than x.

blocks.question: Which one is a valid variable name?
blocks.explain: high_score uses snake_case, has no spaces, and doesn't start with a number. The others break a rule.

blocks.heading: The four core data types 🧩
blocks.html:
Every value in Python has a type — what kind of thing it is. Right now we care about four:

  • int — whole numbers, like 7 or -3.
  • float — numbers with a decimal point, like 3.14 or 0.5.
  • str — text (a "string"), always inside quotes, like "hello".
  • bool — a yes/no value: either True or False (capital first letter!).

blocks.caption: One variable of each type
blocks.code:
lives = 3
price = 2.50
name = "Robo"
is_fun = True

print(lives)
print(price)
print(name)
print(is_fun)
blocks.explain: lives is an int, price is a float, name is a str (in quotes), and is_fun is a bool. Four boxes, four kinds of stuff.

blocks.heading: Peeking at a type with type()
blocks.html: Not sure what kind of value you have? Ask Python! Wrap a value in type() and it tells you the type.

blocks.caption: Asking Python what type things are
blocks.explain: Python answers with <class 'int'>, <class 'float'>, <class 'str'>, and <class 'bool'>. The word after 'class' is the type name.

blocks.question: What type is the value 4.0?
blocks.explain: Even though it looks like a whole number, the decimal point makes 4.0 a float.

blocks.question: Which value is a string (str)?
blocks.explain: The quotes around "42" make it text — a string — not a number you can do math with.

blocks.heading: Reassigning: putting new stuff in the box 🔄
blocks.html: A variable isn't stuck with its first value. You can put something new in the box anytime, and the old value is replaced.

blocks.caption: Changing a variable's value
blocks.explain: First the box holds 0. Then we put 100 in, which throws out the old 0. Python only remembers the latest value.

blocks.html: Watch the capitals on booleans: True and False must start with a capital letter. Writing true will give you an error.

blocks.heading: Combining variables in print()
blocks.html: You can print several things in one line by separating them with commas inside print(). Python puts a space between each one automatically.

blocks.caption: Printing text and variables together
blocks.code:
name = "Sam"
age = 11
print("Hi, my name is", name, "and I am", age, "years old.")
blocks.explain: The commas glue text and variables into one friendly sentence, with neat spaces in between.

blocks.title: Make a 'me' card
blocks.instructions: Create three variables: my_name (your name in quotes), my_age (a whole number), and likes_python (set it to True). Then print all three. Press Check when your output shows your name.
blocks.starter:
my_name = "___"
my_age = 0
likes_python = True
print(my_name)
print(my_age)
print(likes_python)
blocks.solution:
my_name = "Thanvish"
my_age = 11
likes_python = True
print(my_name)
print(my_age)
print(likes_python)
blocks.passMsg: Nice — three variables, three types, all printed. You're getting it! 🎉
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you have variables named my_name, my_age, and likes_python, and that likes_python is True.

blocks.title: Check a type
blocks.instructions: Make a variable called temperature and set it to a decimal number like 98.6. Then print its type using type(). Your output should mention 'float'.
blocks.passMsg: You asked Python for the type and it said float — exactly right! 🌡️
blocks.failMsg: Set temperature to a decimal number (like 98.6) and print type(temperature).

challenge.title: Build a player profile
challenge.instructions: Create a tiny game profile using variables. Make: player_name (text), level (a whole number), health (a decimal like 99.5), and is_alive (True or False). Then print one friendly line that uses all of them, like: Player Robo is on level 3 with 99.5 health. Alive: True
challenge.starter:
player_name = "Robo"
level = 1
health = 100.0
is_alive = True
# print one line that uses all four variables
challenge.solution:
player_name = "Robo"
level = 3
health = 99.5
is_alive = True
print("Player", player_name, "is on level", level, "with", health, "health. Alive:", is_alive)
challenge.hint: Use one print() with commas between the text pieces and the four variables: print("Player", player_name, ...).
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 What a profile! You stored four different types and printed them in one line — Day 2 complete. You're leveling up fast!
